coredata.dc:title: Facile, ultrafast synthesis of up- and down-conversion fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive detection of hematin
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Zhi-yong
Duo, Shu-wang
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are promising zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with semiconductor-like properties, which have attracted tremendous attention owing to their fascinating features such as excellent optical performance, chemical inertness, and low toxicology. In this investigation, Mg-doped CDs (Mg-CDs) with up- and down-conversion fluorescence (FL) were prepared within 2 min through a one-pot, facile and ultrafast microwave method. The obtained Mg-CDs showed excitation-independent blue emission and were potentially applied for the detection of hematin based on the strong inner filter effect (IFE). The FL intensity ratio (I 0/I) of the synthesized Mg-CDs were proportional to the concentration of hematin in the range of 0.1–75 μM with the limit of detection of 25 nM for Mg-CDs-0.1 and 0.01–25 μM with the limit of detection of 1 nM for Mg-CDs-0.5. Overall, this selective and sensitive assay using Mg-CDs obtained through the current microwave method was believed to demonstrate significant application prospects in complex matrices owing to its simplicity and specificity for hematin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microwave synthesis
Hematin detection
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and purification of N-CDs and Mg-CDs
Standard operating procedure for detecting hematin
Synthesis and characterization of N-CDs and Mg-CDs
Optical properties of N-CDs and Mg-CDs
Detection of hematin using the as-prepared Mg-CDs-0.1 and Mg-CDs-0.5
Analysis of hematin in human red cells hemolysate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology of the as-prepared N-CDs and Mg-CDs-0.1. (a) HRTEM image and (b) particle size distribution of N-CDs. (c) HRTEM image and (d) particle size distribution of Mg-CDs-0.1.
Elemental composition of the N-CDs and Mg-CDs. XPS spectra of (a) N-CDs, (b) Mg-CDs-0.1 and (c) Mg-CDs-0.5. (d) Percentage of Mg2+ ions in three kinds of CDs.

The optical properties of CDs. The absorption spectra of (a) N-CDs, (b) Mg-CDs-0.1 and (c) Mg-CDs-0.5. The maximum excitation and emission spectra of (d) N-CDs, (e) Mg-CDs-0.1 and (f) Mg-CDs-0.5. The up-conversion emission spectra of (g) N-CDs, (h) Mg-CDs-0.1 and (i) Mg-CDs-0.5.

(a) FL intensity of Mg-CDs-0.1 in the absence and presence of hematin (500 μM) (b) The sensitivity of hematin detection using Mg-CDs-0.1 (pH, 7.4; λ ex, 314 nm) (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of hematin and the excitation and emission spectra of Mg-CDs-0.1 (d) FL decay profiles of Mg-CDs-0.1 in the absence and presence of hematin (e) UV–Vis absorption spectra of hematin and the excitation and emission spectra of Mg-CDs-0.5 (f) The sensitivity of hematin detection using Mg-CDs-0.5 (pH, 7.4; λ ex, 362 nm).
Illustration of the one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis of Mg-CDs and the highly selective and sensitive detection of hematin.
Determination of hematin in healthy human red cells. aAverage of three replicate measurements.

head.ce:title: Facile, ultrafast synthesis of up- and down-conversion fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive detection of hematin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Surface Engineering, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Jiangxi, Nanchang, 330013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Key Laboratory of Surface Engineering
Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Key laboratory of Surface Engineering, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Jiangxi, Nanchang, 330013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Vocational College of Science and Technology, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Vocational College of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi vocational college of science and technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are promising zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with semiconductor-like properties, which have attracted tremendous attention owing to their fascinating features such as excellent optical performance, chemical inertness, and low toxicology. In this investigation, Mg-doped CDs (Mg-CDs) with up- and down-conversion fluorescence (FL) were prepared within 2 min through a one-pot, facile and ultrafast microwave method. The obtained Mg-CDs showed excitation-independent blue emission and were potentially applied for the detection of hematin based on the strong inner filter effect (IFE). The FL intensity ratio (I 0/I) of the synthesized Mg-CDs were proportional to the concentration of hematin in the range of 0.1–75 μM with the limit of detection of 25 nM for Mg-CDs-0.1 and 0.01–25 μM with the limit of detection of 1 nM for Mg-CDs-0.5. Overall, this selective and sensitive assay using Mg-CDs obtained through the current microwave method was believed to demonstrate significant application prospects in complex matrices owing to its simplicity and specificity for hematin.

In this work, we developed a highly sensitive method of detecting hematin by Mg-CDs based on the strong inner filter effect.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), a new form of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, exhibit numerous fascinating features such as superior electronic properties, broad absorption range, excellent biocompatibility, low-cost, and good chemical stability [1–5]. Due to their superior optical properties, CDs are considered to be a new type of fluorescent nanoprobe, displaying promising application prospects compared to inorganic semiconductor quantum dots and organic fluorescent dyes [6–8]. The good water solubility of CDs is due to the oxygen-containing groups (carboxyl or hydroxyl) on their surface. Meanwhile, strong photobleaching resistance ensures CDs for long-term cell imaging. Therefore, CDs are widely applied in the fields of chemo-and bio-sensing [6,9], cellular imaging [10,11], targeted cancer therapy [12,13] and catalysis [14,15]. Currently, their importance can be understood from many published review articles that summarize the research progress of CDs. Huang et al. [6] described the green synthesis of CDs, including biomass synthesis and sustainable synthesis techniques. Furthermore, the same group [16] summarized the functionalization strategies of CDs, including heteroatom doping and surface modification. Moreover, Kailasa et al. [17] indicated about recent studies on CDs for the development of neuro drug delivery systems to reach the central nervous system via crossing blood-brain barrier. Das et al. [18] reported on the potential applications of green CDs in the fields of bioimaging, drug/gene delivery systems, catalysis, and sensing.

Heteroatom doping, including metal and non-metal doping, is considered to be a simple and effective functionalization strategy for producing highly fluorescent CDs by adjusting the intrinsic structure and electron distribution in the CDs [19]. The luminescent efficiency of CDs is found to be dependent on the amounts and types of heteroatom dopants. Generally, non-metallic doping such as N-doping or S-doping can effectively improve the FL quantum yield (QY) of CDs, as electrons trapped by the newly formed N-state present high FL QY [20]. Metallic doping such as Cu-doping or Gd-doping can be applied to modulate the band structure of CDs. Moreover, metallic dopants can prevent overconsumption of amino and carboxyl groups during dehydration and carbonization through chelation between metal ions and chemical groups [16].

Hematin (Fe3+ protoporphyrin IX liganded to OH−) represents the oxidized form of free heme, which is the prosthetic group of haemoglobin [21]. Hematin not only induces lysis of human erythrocytes but also produces redox reactive substances such as hydroxyl and superoxide radicals, which directly mediate lipid peroxidation through different mechanisms [22–24]. An increase in the hematin level leads to various inflammatory lesions, such as vascular disorders [25]. Moreover, the binding of hematin with the red cell membrane can also cause membrane damage [26]. Although high-performance liquid chromatography [27], mass spectrometry [28], absorption spectrophotometry [29] and electrochemical method [30] have been developed for the determination of hematin, they suffer from lengthy procedures, low sensitivity or expensive. Considering the toxicity of hematin, it necessitates developing a facile, highly sensitive, and selective detection assay for hematin.

Ethylenediamine (EDA) and hematin were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Citric acid and magnesium chloride (MgCl2) were commercially available from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The water used in all experiments was purified with a Millipore system.

The absorption spectra of CDs were obtained from a Hitachi U-3010 spectrophotometer. The FL spectra of CDs were recorded with a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The element compositions of CDs were measured with an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The FT-IR spectra of CDs were collected on a Hitachi FTIR-8400S Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) data of the CDs was performed on a Tecnai G2 F20 field emission transmission electron microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification of N-CDs and Mg-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs could be prepared through a simple one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis. In a typical protocol, 0.5 g citric acid was dissolved with 5 mL MgCl2 solution (0.1 M), and then mixed with 0.1 mL EDA. After shaking up, the reaction solution was transferred to microwave oven (1000 W) for heating. The Mg-CDs-0.1 could be rapid synthesized within 2 min. For comparison, N-CDs and Mg-CDs-0.5 could be obtained through using distilled water or 0.5 M MgCl2 solution instead of 0.1 M MgCl2 solution, while other processes were consistent with the above microwave synthesis. Residual amounts of citric acid, MgCl2 and EDA were removed through a cellulose ester dialysis membrane (500–1000 MWCO) over 48 h. The resulting material was dried by lyophilization, which were dispersed in water for further characterization and use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard operating procedure for detecting hematin
ce:section.ce:para: Titration of Mg-CDs with hematin was made by adding various concentrations of hematin (0–75 μM) into the Mg-CDs-0.1 (10 μg/mL in PBS buffer, pH = 7.4) or adding various concentrations of hematin (0–25 μM) into Mg-CDs-0.5 solution (10 μg/mL in PBS buffer, pH = 7.4), and then subjected to FL measurements after 5 min. The FL spectra were recorded under excitation at 314 nm for Mg-CDs-0.1 and at 362 nm for Mg-CDs-0.5, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of N-CDs and Mg-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The microwave-assisted synthesis has been used for the preparation of CDs, as the process is simple and time-saving, and causes homogeneous and efficient heating [31]. The CDs were rapidly synthesized within 2 min. HRTEM image (Fig. 1 a) shows that the size of N-CDs is in the range from 1.0 to 4.0 nm, with an average size of 2.5 nm (Fig. 1b). Similarly, the size of Mg-CDs-0.1 is 2.0 nm in diameter with narrow distribution (Fig. 1c and d). Both N-CDs and Mg-CDs-0.1 exist as amorphous carbon particles without any crystal lattices, which is consistent with the reported results. Moreover, Raman spectra were used to analyze the crystallinity of CDs. CDs displayed two characteristic peaks at about 1365 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 ( Fig. S1 , ESI), which correspond to D and G bands, respectively. The intensity ratio (I D/I G) of D band (disorder) to G band (crystalline) was often used to determine the crystallinity of the carbon core. The larger I D/I G values of N-CDs (I D/I G = 0.99) and Mg-CDs-0.1 (I D/I G = 1.11) indicate an increase in the amount of disordered carbon within N-CDs and Mg-CDs-0.1 [16]. The FT-IR spectra ( Fig. S2 , ESI) of N-CDs and Mg-CDs-0.1 exhibited the characteristic absorption bands of O–H/N–H, CO, CN and C–N stretching vibrations at 3430 cm−1, 1710 cm−1, 1657 cm−1, and 1382 cm−1, respectively [32]. The characteristic absorption band of N–H bending vibration could be noted at 1558 cm−1 [33]. Meanwhile, the peak positions observed from the FT-IR spectrum (red curve) of N-CDs were comparable to the FT-IR spectrum (black curve) of Mg-CDs-0.1, indicating that they possess similar surface groups. The difference in the peak intensities may be due to the change in the dipole moments from the chelation of Mg2+ ions with carboxyl groups [32].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used further to confirm the doping of Mg2+ ions into CDs. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 a) of N-CDs shows C1s, N1s, and O1s peaks located at about 285.5, 400.8, and 532.1 eV, respectively [34–36]. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b) of Mg-CDs-0.1 displays C1s, N1s, O1s, and Mg2s peaks located at about 284.8, 400.2, 532.4, and 99.2 eV, respectively, indicating the successful introduction of Mg2+ ions into CDs [32,33]. Furthermore, the peak appearing at 307.8 eV in the XPS spectrum of Mg-CDs-0.5 ( Fig. 2c and Fig. S3 , ESI) could be attributed to the Mg KLL bond. Mg contents of 0.45% and 1.3% were detected from Mg-CDs-0.1 and Mg-CDs-0.5, respectively ( Fig. 2d), signifying the successful introduction of Mg2+ ions into CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of N-CDs and Mg-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to π–π* transition of the aromatic ring structure (aromatic CC bonds) and n–π* transition of CO bond in the carbon cores [37–39], the as-prepared N-CDs exhibit two characteristic absorption bands at about 241 and 354 nm (Fig. 3 a). The absorption spectrum (Fig. 3b) of Mg-CDs-0.1 does not show any apparent change compared to N-CDs since only a small amount of Mg atoms in Mg-CDs-0.1 does not affect the internal structure of CDs. However, the π–π* transition caused by the aromatic ring structure in the Mg-CDs-0.5 disappeared when 0.5 M MgCl2 was introduced into CDs (Fig. 3c). This could be due to that a large number of Mg2+ ions could dope into CDs, which in turn destroy the carbon core. Affected by Mg2+ ions, the absorption peak corresponding to n–π* transition of Mg-CDs-0.5 caused a blue-shift from 355 to 340 nm as compared to N-CDs, which further confirms the successful introduction of Mg2+ ions into CDs.

Furthermore, N-CDs revealed excitation-independent FL emissions, with the maximum emission and maximum excitation, were at 459 nm and 300 nm, respectively ( Fig. 3d and Fig. S4a , ESI). Whereas, Mg-CDs-0.1 exhibited excitation-independent FL emissions and the maximum emission and maximum excitation were at 459 nm and 314 nm, respectively ( Fig. 3e and Fig. S4b , ESI). Moreover, Mg-CDs-0.5 presented excitation-independent FL emissions and the maximum emission, and maximum excitation were at 443 nm and 362 nm, respectively ( Fig. 3f and Fig. S4c , ESI). The excitation-independent FL emissions imply the uniform size or surface state of three CDs possessed [40]. However, the maximum excitation wavelength of Mg-CDs-0.5 appeared to red-shift from 300 to 362 nm as compared to N-CDs, which could be attributed to the introduction of Mg2+ ions into CDs.

Alternatively, the Stokes shift of CDs FL decreased gradually with an increase in the content of Mg2+ ions in CDs. Therefore, Mg2+ ions could significantly change the optical properties of CDs by changing its internal structure. Importantly, the doping of Mg2+ ions significantly increased the FL QY of CDs from 10.1 to 61.6% ( Fig. S5 , ESI). The high QY and strong blue FL of Mg-CDs-0.5 suggest that both N and Mg elements play a critical role in the FL enhancement since N atoms are strong electron donors and thus could enhance the conjugated π-domains of Mg-CDs-0.5. Meanwhile, Mg2+ ions as a chelation agent protect the carboxyl groups of citric acid during dehydration and carbonization [16].

Also, N-CDs exhibited very weak up-conversion emissions when excited from 680 nm to 750 nm (Fig. 3g). Remarkably, Mg-CDs-0.1, especially Mg-CDs-0.5, showed good up-conversion properties (Fig. 3h and i). Earlier, few studies had reported CDs with up-conversion FL, and methods were developed to enhance the up-conversion FL. Herein, the strong up-conversion FL of CDs could be easily achieved by doping Mg2+ ions into CDs. The up-conversion FL spectra of Mg-CDs-0.1 and Mg-CDs-0.5 demonstrated that the wavelengths of up-conversion emission peaks are located at about 459 nm and 443 nm, when excited at 620 nm and 700 nm, respectively.

We further investigated the effect of Mg2+ ions on the stability of CDs. Stability of nanoparticles was one important property, which could avoid false positive/negative results in chemical sensing [41]. The as-prepared Mg-CDs-0.1 exhibited superior photobleaching resistance, and the FL intensity hardly changed under continuous ultraviolet irradiation within 150 min (
Fig. S6
, ESI). Moreover, the Mg-CDs-0.1 were very stable in a salty medium. The FL intensity of Mg-CDs-0.1 changed slightly, even if in a medium with an NaCl concentration as high as 4 M ( Fig. S7 , ESI). Additionally, the Mg-CDs-0.1 were found to be relatively stable under a broad pH range ( Fig. S8 , ESI). Therefore, the Mg-CDs-0.1 had good stability, which were beneficial for hematin detection in complex conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of hematin using the as-prepared Mg-CDs-0.1 and Mg-CDs-0.5
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the FL quenching of Mg-CDs-0.1 in the presence of hematin, an optical sensor for the detection of hematin in solution has been developed. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the FL of Mg-CDs-0.1 could be efficiently quenched when the concentration of hematin was 500 μM. To test the sensitivity of Mg-CDs-0.1 nanoprobe, different concentrations of hematin in the range of 0–150 μM were investigated. A gradual decrease in the FL at 459 nm with an increase in the concentration of hematin indicates the sensitivity of the system to the concentration of hematin. The FL intensity ratio (I 0/I) of the Mg-CDs-0.1 was proportional to the concentration of hematin in the range of 0.1–75 μM with a LOD of 25 nM (3σ/K) ( Fig. 4b).

The FL quenching with high-efficiency of Mg-CDs-0.1 by hematin is due to the strong IFE between them. The hematin showed strong absorption at about 390 nm (ε = 5.4 × 104 cm−1 M−1) and exhibited large overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of Mg-CDs-0.1 ( Fig. 4c). Fig. 4d illustrates the FL decay curves for Mg-CDs-0.1 in the absence and presence of hematin. The lifetime of Mg-CDs-0.1 was 5.17 ns, which did not change after adding hematin into Mg-CDs-0.1 solution (5.01 ns). This further confirms the strong IFE between Mg-CDs-0.1 and hematin [42,43]. To further confirm the determination of hematin based on IFE, Mg-CDs-0.5 were used to detect hematin due to better spectral overlap between Mg-CDs-0.5 and hematin (Fig. 4e). The FL intensity ratio (I 0/I) of the Mg-CDs-0.5 was noted to be proportional to the concentration of hematin in the range of 0.01–25 μM with a LOD of 1 nM (3σ/K) (Fig. 4f).

In addition, the FL quenching efficiency had been analyzed by Stern−Volmer equation [44]:
I 0/I = K SV[Q] + 1
where I 0 and I were FL intensities before and after the addition of the hematin, [Q] was the molar concentration of hematin and K SV was the quenching constant (M−1). In this study, the FL intensity ratio of Mg-CDs-0.1 in the presence of hematin was linear with the concentration of hematin with the Stern–Volmer quenching constant of K SV = 3 × 104 M−1 (when [Q] was 1 μM, K SV = (I 0/I – 1)/[Q] = (0.01 × 1 + 1.02 − 1)/(1 × 10−6) = 3 × 104 M−1). Meanwhile, the FL intensity ratio of Mg-CDs-0.5 in the presence of hematin was also linear with the concentration of hematin with the Stern–Volmer quenching constant of K SV = 4.5 × 104 M−1 (when [Q] was 15 μM, K SV = (I 0/I – 1)/[Q] = (0.04 × 15 + 1.08 − 1)/(15 × 10−6) = 4.5 × 104 M−1).

The K SV could be further analyzed to determine which type of quenching (static quenching effect (SQE) and dynamic quenching effect (DQE)) was observed in this work. In SQE, K SV was considered as a ground-state association constant for Mg-CDs binding to hematin. However, for DQE, the K SV was part of the following equation [44]:
K SV = k q × τ0
where k q was the quenching rate constant and τ0 is the FL lifetime of Mg-CDs (τ0 = 5.17 ns for Mg-CDs-0.1, τ0 = 6.36 ns for Mg-CDs-0.5, Fig. S9, ESI). The resulted k q had the value of 5.8 × 1012 M−1 s−1 for Mg-CDs-0.1 and 7.1 × 1012 M−1 s−1 for Mg-CDs-0.5, indicating a static quenching rather than a dynamic quenching in accordance that the rate constant of a dynamic quenching was usually less than 1.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1 [45–47].

The FL response specificity of Mg-CDs-0.1 towards hematin over other molecules and metal ions in solution was investigated. After treating Mg-CDs-0.1 solution with interfering reagents, the FL intensity at 459 nm was measured. As shown in Fig. S10, a significant decrease in the FL intensity, was observed after the reaction of Mg-CDs-0.1 with hematin. In contrast, no apparent changes in the emission were detected after the reaction of Mg-CDs-0.1 with other molecules or ions. Meanwhile, the interfering reagents, including different molecules and metal ions, also found to have no influence on the FL intensity of Mg-CDs-0.1 in the presence of hematin. Similarly, the hematin-induced fluorescent quenching of Mg-CDs-0.5 was also highly specific ( Fig. S11 , ESI). Therefore, the proposed method is confirmed to demonstrate good selectivity for the detection of hematin. A comparison is made with the results obtained from other reported methods ( Table S1 , ESI). Although the LOD of the current one is not the lowest, the high selectivity, wider linear range, and simplicity make it novel for the detection of hematin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of hematin in human red cells hemolysate
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was applied for the detection of hematin in healthy human red cell hemolysate. Before detection, the hemolysate was prepared based on a reported method [48]. Firstly, 20 mL of isotonic NaCl solution (0.9%) was added to 2 mL of a blood sample from healthy adults, and then the tube was gently agitated to homogenize the suspension. To remove the supernatant, the mixture was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. After washing three times and repeating the above process, the red blood cells were dispersed into 25 mL of ultrapure water. Because of different osmotic pressures between water and red cells, the red cells ruptured. To compress the cell membranes, the suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm. Finally, the hemolysate was obtained and diluted 100-fold using ultrapure water before use.

Following this, the hemolysate samples spiked with different concentrations of standard hematin solution (1, 10, 25 μM) were measured in the presence of Mg-CDs-0.1 or Mg-CDs-0.5. The reliability and accuracy of the analysis were evaluated via a recovery study, as listed in Table 1 . The calculations of standard deviation (SD) and relative standard deviation (RSD) were based on the following Eqns. (1) and (2) [49]:
(1)
S D =
∑ i n
(
x i
−
x n
)
2
n − 1
(2)
R S D
=
S D
x n
×
100 %

The obtained recoveries of the two samples varied from 92.8% to 106.2% for Mg-CDs-0.1 and from 95.5% to 105.3% for Mg-CDs-0.5, revealing the excellent application prospects of this method in the detection of hematin owing to its simplicity and specificity.

In conclusion, in this study, a facile and an ultrafast microwave method was developed to synthesise Mg-CDs. The doping of Mg efficiently enhanced the FL QY of CDs as Mg-citric acid chelate introduces Mg atom into CDs and preserves the carboxyl group. Moreover, Mg-CDs could act as superior nanoprobes for highly selective and sensitive detection of hematin based on the strong IFE between Mg-CDs and hematin. Furthermore, Mg-CDs demonstrated high reproducibility and selectivity for the detection of hematin in complex samples.
Zhi-yong Yu: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Shu-wang Duo: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from the financial support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41763020), the Jing Gang Scholars Programme of Jiangxi Province, the Foundation of Jiangxi Educational Commission (No. GJJ180596).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Terbium (III) modified fluorescent carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive ratiometry of stringent
